items.question_text: Under 2-6-1, controllers must become familiar with _____ weather information when coming on duty.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-1, controllers must become familiar with 'pertinent' weather information when coming on duty and stay aware of current and forecasted weather needed for ATC duties.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-2, what are the two types of PIREPs that require emphasis in solicitation and dissemination?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2, emphasis must be placed on solicitation and dissemination of Urgent (UUA) and Routine (UA) PIREPs. These are the two formal PIREP categories requiring controller attention.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-2, which of the following would warrant a PIREP that controllers should disseminate?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2, PIREPs include reports of volcanic eruptions and volcanic ash clouds among other conditions pertinent to flight safety such as thunderstorms, icing, wind shear, turbulence, and braking action.

choices.text: A radio frequency change requested by the pilot.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-3, when the prevailing visibility at the tower level is less than _____ miles, tower personnel must take prevailing visibility observations.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-3a, tower personnel must take prevailing visibility observations when the prevailing visibility at the usual point of observation, or at the tower level, is less than 4 miles.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-3, the wind is described as calm when the wind velocity is less than _____ knots.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-3b, describe the wind as calm when the wind velocity is less than three knots. This is an operational definition all controllers must know.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-3, when both tower and surface observations are available, which visibility value is used for aircraft operations?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-3a1, the lower of the two observations (tower or surface) must be used for aircraft operations. This ensures the most conservative visibility value is applied for safety.

choices.text: The surface observation, since it is at ground level.
The tower observation, since it has a wider view.
The lower of the two observations.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-4, weather and chaff areas must be defined to potentially affected aircraft in terms of azimuth using the _____ clock and distance from the aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-4a1, areas are defined in terms of azimuth by referring to the '12-hour clock' and distance from the aircraft. This is the standard spatial reference for weather/chaff advisories.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-5, which type of weather information may be transmitted to pilots WITHOUT consulting the weather reporting station?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-5a, general weather information not including specific values (such as 'large breaks in the overcast') and elements derived from instruments, pilots, or radar may be transmitted without consulting the weather station. Specific values like ceiling and visibility require certification or weather station consultation.

choices.text: General weather information and instrument-derived elements.
Only information obtained from the official ATIS broadcast.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-6, controllers must advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within _____ NM of their sector.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-6, controllers must advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within 150 NM of their sector or area of jurisdiction. This defines the geographic scope of the advisory obligation.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-6, which of the following is NOT listed as a type of hazardous weather advisory that controllers must use?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-6, hazardous weather advisories include AIRMET (except over CONUS), SIGMET, Convective SIGMET (WST), Urgent Pilot Weather Reports (UUA), and Center Weather Advisories (CWA). TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast product, not an advisory for hazardous weather.

items.situation: You are working the tower. The surface visibility observation reports 5 miles of prevailing visibility, but from the tower cab you observe that the visibility at tower level is approximately 3 miles due to a low cloud layer that partially obscures your view.
items.instruction: Under 2-6-3, what actions must you take?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-3a, when tower-level visibility is less than 4 miles, tower personnel must: (1) use the lower of the two observations (tower or surface) for aircraft operations — so 3 miles would be used, (2) forward the tower observation to the weather observer, and (3) notify the weather observer of the visibility decrease below 4 miles.

choices.text: Use the 3-mile tower observation, forward it to the weather observer, and notify the observer of the decrease below 4 miles.
Use the 5-mile surface observation since it is the official weather reporting station value.
Average the two observations and use 4 miles for operations.
Only forward the observation; surface visibility governs aircraft operations regardless.

items.instruction: You receive a SIGMET for severe turbulence covering an area 200 NM east of your sector boundary. The affected area is well outside your 150 NM advisory radius.
items.question_text: Under 2-6-6, are you required to broadcast this hazardous weather advisory on your frequencies?
items.explanation: No. Per 2-6-6, advisories are not required if aircraft on your frequency(s) will not be affected. The 150 NM radius defines the 'may impact' zone. Since the SIGMET area is 200 NM away and outside your area of impact, and no aircraft on your frequency will be affected, no advisory is required.

choices.text: No, because the weather is outside the 150 NM area and no aircraft on frequency will be affected.
Yes, because all SIGMETs must be broadcast regardless of distance from your sector.
Yes, because the 200 NM distance only reduces the urgency, not the obligation.
No, because SIGMETs only apply within the CONUS.
